text:
DEPARTMENT OF PATHOLOGY AND LABORATORY MEDICINE
SURGICAL PATHOLOGY REPORT

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PATIENT: Raymond Kowalski
MRN: [REDACTED]
ACCESSION NUMBER: SP-[REDACTED]
ORDERING PHYSICIAN: [Attending Oncologist], MD — Medical Oncology
PERFORMING PATHOLOGIST: [Staff Pathologist], MD — Gastrointestinal/Hepatobiliary Pathology
CLINICAL INDICATION: 71-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), Gleason 9 (5+4), BRCA2 germline mutation, known osseous and pelvic nodal metastases. New hepatic lesions identified on CT and confirmed by gadolinium-enhanced MRI liver (segment VI-VII right hepatic lobe, 1.2 cm and 1.0 cm). Biopsy requested for histologic characterization and confirmation of metastatic disease. Prior treatment history: leuprolide + abiraterone (first line), enzalutamide (second line), olaparib 100 mg BID (reduced dose, third line, ongoing). Bone marrow suppression, transfusion-dependent anemia.
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

SPECIMEN INFORMATION

Specimen ID:         SP-[REDACTED]-A and SP-[REDACTED]-B
Date of Procedure:   [DATE]
Date Received:       [DATE]
Date Reported:       [DATE]
Type of Specimen:    CT-guided percutaneous core needle biopsy, right hepatic lobe (segment VI-VII), dominant lesion (1.2 cm target on intraprocedural CT guidance)
Procedure:           CT-guided liver biopsy, performed by Interventional Radiology
Anesthesia:          Moderate sedation
Clinical History:    As above. Germline BRCA2 p.E1308* confirmed. Prior prostate biopsy (SP-[REDACTED]) showed acinar adenocarcinoma, Gleason pattern 5+4=9, Grade Group 5, with extensive intraductal carcinoma. PSA at time of biopsy: 11.8 ng/mL (rising). ALP: 287 U/L. LDH: 412 U/L.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

GROSS DESCRIPTION

Received in formalin, labeled with patient name and "liver biopsy core A" and "liver biopsy core B," are two tan-pink, cylindrical soft tissue core biopsy specimens.

  Core A: Measures 1.4 cm in length x 0.15 cm in diameter. The cut surface appears homogenous, pale tan-white. Submitted entirely in one cassette (A1).

  Core B: Measures 1.5 cm in length x 0.15 cm in diameter. The tissue is similarly pale tan, with focal areas of slightly firmer, whitish tissue suggestive of infiltrative tumor. Submitted entirely in one cassette (B1).

No normal hepatic parenchyma with recognizable trabecular architecture is grossly appreciated in either core. No bile, necrotic material, or calcification noted grossly. Representative sections entirely submitted.

Cassettes: A1 (core A, entirely submitted), B1 (core B, entirely submitted). Total cassettes: 2.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION

Sections from both cores (A1 and B1) show hypercellular tissue composed of infiltrating epithelial neoplasm. The background hepatic parenchyma is largely replaced or compressed by tumor, with rare residual hepatocytes seen at the periphery of core B showing mild steatosis and non-specific reactive changes.

The neoplasm is composed predominantly of poorly differentiated glandular structures and solid nests/sheets of epithelial cells. Individual cells demonstrate enlarged, pleomorphic nuclei with prominent nucleoli, scant to moderate amphophilic cytoplasm, and an increased nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio. Cribriform and fused glandular architecture is focally present, consistent with Gleason pattern 5 morphology in the context of prostate primary. True acinar formation, where identifiable, is markedly distorted. Scattered individual tumor cells and small cell clusters are present in a desmoplastic stromal background.

High-grade cytologic features are prominent: mitotic figures are readily identified (approximately 8-10 per 10 high power fields), including atypical forms. Apoptotic bodies are scattered throughout the tumor.

FOCAL FINDING: A minor component (estimated <5% of total tumor cells in the submitted cores) demonstrates cells with more plasmacytoid, rounded nuclei, finely granular chromatin, indistinct nucleoli, and scant cytoplasm, arranged in loose nests without glandular formation. This morphology raises the possibility of focal neuroendocrine differentiation, which is further evaluated by ancillary studies (see below).

No lymphovascular invasion is identified in the limited biopsy material. No perineural invasion is identified. There is no lymphoid aggregate or granulomatous inflammation to suggest an infectious etiology. No hemorrhagic necrosis or geographic necrosis identified within this limited sampling.

Residue liver parenchyma (core B, peripheral): residual hepatocytes show compressed, atrophied trabeculae with minimal reactive fibrosis at the tumor-liver interface. No cirrhotic nodules, no bile duct obstruction. Portal tracts show mild chronic inflammatory infiltrate.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ANCILLARY STUDIES

IMMUNOHISTOCHEMISTRY (IHC) — performed on block A1 with appropriate positive and negative controls:

  Prostate Lineage Markers:
  - PSA (polyclonal):         POSITIVE — focal, weak cytoplasmic and luminal staining in ~40% of tumor cells. Note: PSA immunoreactivity is commonly downregulated in high-grade and post-treatment prostate carcinoma.
  - PSMA (Clone 3E6):         POSITIVE — moderate to strong membranous/cytoplasmic staining, diffuse (>80% of tumor cells). This is the dominant prostate lineage marker retained in this specimen.
  - NKX3.1 (Clone EP356):     POSITIVE — strong nuclear staining, diffuse (>90% of tumor cells). Highly specific for prostatic origin.
  - Androgen Receptor (AR, Clone SP107): POSITIVE — moderate nuclear staining, approximately 60-70% of tumor cells. Heterogeneous distribution, with some tumor cell clusters showing diminished or absent AR expression.
  - Prostein (P501S):         POSITIVE — granular cytoplasmic staining, focal (~30% of cells). Consistent with prostatic origin.

  Neuroendocrine Markers:
  - Synaptophysin (Clone MRQ-40): FOCALLY POSITIVE — weak to moderate cytoplasmic granular positivity in approximately 3-5% of tumor cells, corresponding to the morphologically plasmacytoid cell population noted on H&E.
  - Chromogranin A (Clone LK2H10): NEGATIVE — no staining identified above background.
  - CD56 (Clone 123C3):       NEGATIVE.
  - INSM1 (Clone A-8):        NEGATIVE — an important negative; absence of INSM1 supports that frank small cell/neuroendocrine carcinoma is not the dominant diagnosis at this time.
  - SOX2:                     Focally positive, weak (approximately 5% of cells).

  Exclusionary Markers:
  - CK7 (Clone OV-TL 12/30):  NEGATIVE.
  - CK20 (Clone Ks20.8):      NEGATIVE.
  - CDX2 (Clone CDX2-88):     NEGATIVE.
  - TTF-1 (Clone 8G7G3/1):    NEGATIVE.
  - HepPar-1 (Clone OCH1E5):  NEGATIVE.
  - Arginase-1:               NEGATIVE.
  - GATA3 (Clone L50-823):    NEGATIVE.
  - p63:                      NEGATIVE.

  Proliferation Index:
  - Ki-67 (Clone MIB-1):      POSITIVE — approximately 55-60% nuclear labeling index (markedly elevated; compare to Ki-67 ~35% on initial prostate biopsy). Increased proliferative index consistent with disease evolution and/or treatment-related clonal selection.

  PTEN (Clone 6H2.1):         LOSS OF EXPRESSION — complete absence of PTEN protein in tumor cells; internal endothelial and stromal cells serve as adequate positive internal controls. Consistent with PTEN homozygous deletion identified on prior NGS.

  RB1 (Clone G3-245):         LOSS OF EXPRESSION — absent/markedly reduced Rb1 nuclear staining in tumor cells (retained in stromal and vascular endothelial cells). Consistent with RB1 loss on prior NGS.

  RESULTS SUMMARY TABLE:
  -------------------------------------------------------
  Marker          Result          Pattern
  -------------------------------------------------------
  PSA             Positive (focal) Cytoplasmic, weak
  PSMA            Positive        Membranous/cytoplasmic, diffuse
  NKX3.1          Positive        Nuclear, diffuse
  AR              Positive        Nuclear, moderate, heterogeneous
  Prostein        Positive        Granular cytoplasmic, focal
  Synaptophysin   Focally Positive Cytoplasmic, <5% cells
  Chromogranin A  Negative        —
  CD56            Negative        —
  INSM1           Negative        —
  SOX2            Focally Positive Nuclear, <5% cells
  CK7             Negative        —
  CK20            Negative        —
  CDX2            Negative        —
  TTF-1           Negative        —
  HepPar-1        Negative        —
  Arginase-1      Negative        —
  GATA3           Negative        —
  p63             Negative        —
  Ki-67           ~55-60%         Nuclear
  PTEN            Loss            —
  RB1             Loss            —
  -------------------------------------------------------

ADDITIONAL ANCILLARY STUDIES — PENDING:
  - Fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) for AR gene amplification: ordered; results pending at time of this report. Results to be issued as addendum.
  - FoundationOne CDx next-generation sequencing: tissue submitted separately from this biopsy block for comprehensive genomic profiling; results pending. To be reported under separate accession.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DIAGNOSTIC FINDINGS

SPECIMEN A (Core A) AND SPECIMEN B (Core B):

METASTATIC ADENOCARCINOMA, CONSISTENT WITH PROSTATIC ORIGIN.

  - Morphology and immunophenotype are consistent with metastatic prostatic acinar adenocarcinoma with high-grade (Gleason pattern 5-equivalent) features in a metastatic site.
  - Strong and diffuse NKX3.1 and PSMA positivity, in conjunction with clinical history of Gleason 9 prostate carcinoma, confirms prostatic primary.
  - Focal synaptophysin positivity (<5% of cells) in the context of absent chromogranin, CD56, and INSM1, combined with retained prostatic lineage marker expression (NKX3.1, PSMA), is most consistent with focal neuroendocrine differentiation (microacinar neuroendocrine features) rather than overt small cell/neuroendocrine carcinoma.
  - The morphologic and immunohistochemical findings, in the setting of RB1 loss, TP53 mutation (per prior genomic testing), and high Ki-67 (~55-60%), are consistent with early treatment-emergent neuroendocrine prostate cancer (t-NEPC) features as a minor component, superimposed on a dominant adenocarcinoma morphology. The diagnosis of frank small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma is NOT supported by the current specimen.
  - Markedly elevated Ki-67 (~55-60%) compared to primary biopsy (~35%) indicates significant increase in proliferative activity, consistent with disease evolution under treatment selective pressure.
  - Loss of PTEN and RB1 protein expression by IHC is concordant with prior genomic data.
  - AR heterogeneity (moderate, ~60-70% of cells) may reflect clonal evolution and AR-dependent and AR-independent tumor subpopulations.

Note: Correlation with AR FISH (pending) and repeat comprehensive genomic profiling (submitted separately, pending) is strongly recommended for complete molecular characterization of metastatic disease and to identify potential resistance mechanisms and therapeutic targets.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

PATHOLOGIST ATTESTATION

The above report was prepared and reviewed by the undersigned attending pathologist. This case was reviewed in conjunction with prior pathologic material (SP-[REDACTED], initial TRUS-guided prostate biopsy, [DATE]) for comparison.

[Staff Pathologist], MD
Department of Pathology and Laboratory Medicine
Division of Gastrointestinal and Hepatobiliary Pathology

Electronic signature applied: [DATE]

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ADDENDUM NOTE (PENDING):
AR FISH and NGS results will be issued as a formal addendum upon receipt. Clinical team notified of pending studies at time of preliminary report.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DISTRIBUTION: Ordering physician (Medical Oncology), Multidisciplinary Tumor Board, Electronic Medical Record.
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
